summary:
The Fall of France in May–June 1940 was the rapid defeat of Allied forces — French, British, Belgian, and Dutch — by Germany in just six weeks, resulting in Germany's occupation of northern and western France and the establishment of the Vichy collaborationist regime in the south. Germany's victory was achieved through an innovative operational plan that concentrated armored forces through the Ardennes forest — terrain considered impassable by Allied planners — outflanking the main Allied defensive positions and cutting off the Allied armies in Belgium from behind. This encirclement trapped over 300,000 Allied troops at Dunkirk, from which they were evacuated in Operation Dynamo.

France signed an armistice on June 22, 1940, with German forces occupying the industrialized north and the Atlantic coast, while the southern zone was governed by the nominally independent Vichy regime under Marshal Philippe Pétain. The armistice terms were designed to humiliate France: it was signed in the same railway carriage in which Germany had signed the [[event:3328|armistice of 1918]].

The speed and totality of France's defeat shocked the world and fundamentally altered the war's trajectory. Britain stood alone against Germany through the summer and autumn of 1940. Charles de Gaulle broadcast from London calling on French people to continue resistance, founding the Free French movement. The Vichy government collaborated with Nazi occupation policies including the deportation of French Jews to death camps. France's defeat remains one of the most studied military catastrophes in modern history, with ongoing debates about the role of strategy, morale, and political will.
